story:
When I was a child, my mother longed for the country, so my father sold his shop, saved for land, and built a farm. The lively sounds of nature made it hard to sleep, and the work was harder than the city, but he learned the land and cared for livestock. I worried for him and helped however I could. I wondered if he regretted it, but when Mom smiled, he smiled; when she laughed, he laughed.

A family moved down the road, and with them came Alya—long black hair, honey-colored eyes, flowy dresses, and a yellow hat I could spot from afar. After chores we walked meadows and wandered the trees. On trips to the city we pooled our allowance for candy and shared it. She was kind and outgoing, drawing me into conversations like no one else could.

When my parents passed, I took over the farm. She came every day until my grief softened. When her parents passed and she inherited their farm, it was my turn to stand beside her. We shared a table—her bread, my soup—and kept each other fed. On slow days we visited the marketplace or treated ourselves to pastries, chatted with old friends and the merchants we delivered to. We still crossed the meadows, and on cold days we sat by the fire. If one of us fell sick, the other took care of both the bedside and the chores.

We kept everything in the open, teasing back and forth. Any bickering ended with laughter. She called me her ball and chain just to make me smile. She was more than a friend; she was my partner. I wanted to make it official, but what could I give her that we hadn’t already shared?

Then I remembered her dream of the ocean—a little house for fishing and swimming, days without worry, salty air and a crisp breeze. I saved every coin from selling tools and making deliveries and bought a cliffside overlook. I tried to build a house myself and failed, so I asked our friends. We learned as we went: mortar that wouldn’t hold, fences and walls that slumped, stones heavy as regret. Sprained ankles, sore backs, and the problem of explaining them. Alya probed. I was never a good liar. We distracted her when she tried to follow. After a year we finished. We pressed the walls, waited, brought an official to certify it. Then I stood at the cliff, wind and spray on my face, and asked her father in heaven for his blessing. It felt like a yes.

I rented a carriage. She was quiet, face turned away, but I caught the blush of a smile. She ran to the little house, yellow hat bright. Is this for me? I hoped it would be for us. She said yes, then paused. We both felt the pull of the farm. We chose to keep it and save the seaside for our off days.

A chaplain married us outside that house with our friends as witnesses. We returned to the fields and, sometimes, the shore. She taught me to fish.

In five years we welcomed four children. We raised them on the farm and took them to the cliff to play. When they were ready to run things, Alya and I moved seaside. Retirement came like quiet surf. Friends visited until they dwindled, and our children and grandchildren filled the rooms. We told our story so often we never forgot it.

Every day we sat together on that cliff and watched the sun rise and set. After she passed, I kept our ritual until my last day. Maybe in heaven a little home waits by the water. If not, I’ll build another. With my friends, for her, I’d do it all over again.
